Title: Evolution of the JULGI–SMXL4/5 module for phloem development in angiosperms
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana and multiple vascular plant species
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: RanBP2-type RNA-binding zinc finger protein with three tandem zinc finger domains
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Coexpressed in phloem tissues across vascular plants from lycophytes to angiosperms
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in fascicular and interfascicular phloem of inflorescence stems
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negatively regulates SMXL4/5 expression and suppresses excessive phloem development
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 loss-of-function mutant analysis; heterologous complementation in arabidopsis jul1 jul2; protoplast GFP reporter assays using SMXL5 5′ UTR; in situ hybridization
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JULGI homologs are evolutionarily conserved in vascular plants and active angiosperm JULGIs rescue the Arabidopsis jul1 jul2 mutant. 2. JULGI suppresses translation through the G-rich 5′ UTR of SMXL4/5 mRNA. 3. Loss of JULGI causes excessive phloem development, showing its role as a negative regulator of phloem formation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: rac-GR24 tested and did not alter JUL1 transcript levels
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: AtJUL1 promoter (native); cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter (transient assay)
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This study concerns vascular development rather than nutrient metabolite accumulation; JULGI is an RNA-binding posttranscriptional regulator, not a metabolic enzyme or transcription factor. The paper identifies SMXL5 as the only confirmed downstream target, while additional JULGI targets are suggested.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: jul1 jul2 mutants show curly and bumpy leaves, short inflorescences, increased branching, and decreased rosette diameter
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate phloem development and source-sink capacity, but direct nutrient metabolite trait evidence is not provided

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana and multiple seed plant species
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: SMXL4/5 homologs are coexpressed in phloem tissues of seed plants
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript level measured in seedlings; not altered by rac-GR24 treatment
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts redundantly with SMXL5 to promote phloem development
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double knockout phenotype interpretation together with complementation framework and expression analyses
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SMXL4 functions with SMXL5 in phloem formation. 2. The JULGI-SMXL4/5 module is independent of canonical strigolactone signaling in angiosperms. 3. SMXL4 transcript level is not changed by rac-GR24 treatment.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core developmental gene but not directly linked to nutrient metabolite biosynthesis in this paper.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss together with SMXL5 causes defective phloem differentiation and short roots
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for vascular development engineering, though nutrient metabolism evidence is absent

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana and multiple seed plant species
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: SMXL4/5 homologs are expressed in phloem tissues; core angiosperm SMXL4/5 contain G-rich 5′ UTRs
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in fascicular and interfascicular phloem of inflorescence stems
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes phloem development; overaccumulation due to removal of the G-rich 5′ UTR element causes enlarged phloem tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR deletion of G-rich 5′ UTR element (Δ39); promoter-driven complementation in smxl4 smxl5; protoplast GFP reporter assays; immunoblotting; in situ hybridization
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SMXL5 is a direct downstream target of JULGI translational repression through a G-rich 5′ UTR element. 2. Elevated SMXL5 translation promotes excessive phloem development. 3. Angiosperm SMXL5 proteins are resistant to rac-GR24-induced degradation, unlike some ancestral/gymnosperm homologs.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study focuses on vascular development rather than nutrient metabolites. Antisense-mediated posttranscriptional silencing involving RDR6 and DCL2 is discussed as background.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overaccumulation causes enlarged phloem tissues and altered leaf/root traits
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target to alter phloem transport capacity and sink strength, but no direct metabolite trait measured in this article
